judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports independently reach the same correct conclusions on all four points with comparable command-level detail; report 2 is marginally more precise by rebasing onto origin/main (the fetched tip) and confirming ancestry via git branch --contains, but the substantive difference is minor.

report_1.evidence: Base branch: `main` (i.e., `origin/main` at `1971f08`). ... Basing the PR on `feat/weighted-scorer` would target a dead branch; main is the only sensible base.

report_2.evidence: Base branch: `main`. The only alternative base would be `feat/weighted-scorer` (the branch it was stacked on), but that branch is effectively dead — its content was already merged into `main` as squash commit `1971f08` ("Refactor scorer for weighted metrics (#42)"). A PR based on `feat/weighted-scorer` would target a branch that will never itself merge, orphaning the PR.

report_1.evidence: `feat/pairwise-metric` was created from the tip of `feat/weighted-scorer` (`ac9d505`, "W3: token-overlap helper in metrics"): its first commit `7b2e100` (X1) has `ac9d505` as parent. That starting point's status relative to main: it has been **squash-merged** into main as `1971f08` (#42) — the commits W1–W3 are not ancestors of main (merge-base of the two branches is still the old base `65204ad`), but their combined content is identical to main's tree (empty `git diff ac9d505 1971f08`).

report_2.evidence: `feat/pairwise-metric` (`7b2e100` X1, `1bfecb6` X2) was created from the tip of `feat/weighted-scorer` (`ac9d505` W3, on top of W1 `d6fdbc3` and W2 `2d3029b`)... `git branch --contains d6fdbc3` lists only the two feature branches — so none of the W commits are ancestors of `main`. However, `git diff feat/weighted-scorer main` is empty: `main`'s tip `1971f08` (#42) is byte-for-byte the same content as W1–W3, i.e. `feat/weighted-scorer` was **squash-merged and is content-dead even though the branch still exists on the remote**.

report_1.evidence: `scoring/scorer.py` — +13/−3 lines ... Not all of it is pairwise-metric work. The `scoring/scorer.py` changes and 6 of the 10 `scoring/metrics.py` lines are the weighted-scorer work (W1–W3) that main **already contains** via the squash-merge. They appear because the squash-merge left the merge-base at `65204ad`, so from git's perspective those commits are "new" on this branch.

report_2.evidence: `scoring/scorer.py` (+13/−3: the weighted-metrics rewrite with `WEIGHTS`). **Not all of it is pairwise-metric work.** ... The `overlap()` function and the entire `scorer.py` change are the inherited W1–W3 commits, which appear in the diff because the merge-base with `main` is `65204ad` — git can't tell that `main` already has this content via the squash commit `1971f08`.

report_1.evidence: Rebase only the pairwise commits onto main, explicitly excluding the squash-merged W commits: `git rebase --onto main ac9d505 feat/pairwise-metric`. ... Verify the result: `git log --oneline main..feat/pairwise-metric` should show exactly two commits (X1, X2), and `git diff main...feat/pairwise-metric` should now equal the 5-line pairwise-only diff.

report_2.evidence: Rebase the branch onto the current trunk tip: `git rebase --onto origin/main feat/weighted-scorer feat/pairwise-metric`. This replays only X1 and X2 onto `main`, dropping the already-merged W commits. ... Verify the result: `git log origin/main..HEAD --oneline` shows exactly X1 and X2, and `git diff origin/main...HEAD` is now only `pairwise_preference` + the grader-prompt line — the diff is scoped to the intended work.
